Metrics: {"retweet_count":13,"reply_count":14,"like_count":157,"quote_count":2,"bookmark_count":297,"impression_count":19506}
Article content: {"media_entities":["3_2038640343617617920","3_2038628070907883520","3_2038641511425060864","3_2038628343537623040","3_2038639923939692545"],"preview_text":"Everyone is vibe coding. That's a problem if you don't split yourself from the pack and be among the 0.1% who do it differently.

That starts from building stuff that 99.9% of vibe coders won't even","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"huggingface.co/papers"},{"text":"connectedpapers.com"}]},"title":"How to Vibe Code Something That Will Make You Rich Rich","plain_text":"Everyone is vibe coding. That's a problem if you don't split yourself from the pack and be among the 0.1% who do it differently.

That starts from building stuff that 99.9% of vibe coders won't even try.
I want to make an argument that might be uncomfortable: the vast majority of vibe-coded apps will never be worth anything. Not because the builders lack talent. 

Because they're building in the most crowded, most replicable, most zero-moat space that has ever existed, and we are only at the beginning of the vibe coding/AI era. 

Less than only 1% of society uses LLMs yet for vibe coding, imagine how things will be when a lot more people are here.

You really gotta differentiate.

Via building stuff that many people will find extremely hard.

This article is about aiming much bigger and adopting a scientific, research-based process to build really hard stuff with a moat.

I'll be sharing here, from direct experience, the process of building harder stuff after more than 12.500 commits. As I've done both the easy and the hard stuff, I can tell you that building easy stuff was just a waste of time (except during the initial learning period).

Yes, some success stories may come from those weekend projects, too, but rarely. 
You wanna be aiming for the harder stuff and build yourself towards that too, luckily there is a way to do that in a disciplined way.
 

Let's begin.

Part I: The Market Nobody Talks About
When AI coding tools dropped the cost of building software to near-zero, the assumption was that this would democratize entrepreneurship. Anyone could build anything. And that's true, anyone CAN build anything.

But not everyone will build something dope.
And not everything will have a moat / a differentiator that is easy to protect. 

If a weekend project can replicate your product, your product isn't defensible. Most products will be competing on marketing, not on substance.
Look at what's actually getting funded, actually getting users, actually creating value. It's not the 500th AI writing assistant. It's things like:
Memory architectures for AI agents that actually persist and learn
Deep research-based infrastructure that will solve a big problem
Domain-specific AI systems that require deep knowledge of a field to even specify/prompt correctly, to be able to get AI to work on it right 
These products have something in common: they're hard to build because they require knowledge that most builders don't have. 

The complexity isn't just in the code. But in the UNDERSTANDING that informs the code. Some things are quite hard for even AI to build, if you're not directing AI right, if you don't know that field well enough. 

And the interesting part is: These are the kind of fields you want to tap into, instead of trying to build that thing a million other people can build now.
This is the gap. And the biggest opportunity in vibe coding right now.
Part II: Research-Driven Vibe Coding
First of all, the strategy isn't "become a PhD researcher." 

You don't need to write papers, or be a professor, or have a background in the thing you'll be building.

BUT
 
You need to READ, in bits if you have to in the beginning, understand the core ideas, and connect the dots until you can translate those ideas into build specifications -> that your LLM can execute.

You may say, "Why don't I just share the research papers directly with my AI tools, and let them do the heavy lifting fully? They can just build it right?"
Well, if that were the case, then we wouldn't call these startups the rather harder ones that 99.9% of the vibe coders won't attempt to build.

Part of the moat is in the fact that:

When you try to give such complex ideas to AI, and if you don't know what you are doing, AI will not produce something great. AI just reflects how much you understand of complex problems and has a hard time going beyond that towards a great solution without your guidance.

You can still use AI to help you with compacting research papers into pieces that you can understand better, make them more compact etc, and that is healthy, however:

You must follow a process of self-growth about the:
> the problem that you're aiming to solve
> how others tried to solve it
> where others ran into problems
> how others came closer to solving it
> where AI is hallucinating when you try to solve it
> and go into the depths of AI's understanding and non-understanding in order to unlock a great product in the end.

While building something pretty hard via vibe coding, you will have to grow towards it; there is no other way, because otherwise someone with a better understanding will come and build something better, because they'll be able to guide the AI better.

And here is the five-phase workflow you must follow for what we call Research-First Vibe Coding (abide by this, give it 100%, and then you'll be surprised at the things you'll be able to ship):
Phase 1: DISCOVER,  Find the Paper That Solves a Real Problem
You start on Hugging Face Daily Papers (huggingface.co/papers), where the AI research community curates the most important new work every day. 

You're not reading everything. You're scanning for papers that solve problems you've personally encountered, papers that introduce new architectures (not just marginal benchmark improvements), and papers at the intersection of two fields.
The intersection papers are the goldmines. Because they will make you think of connected systems more, and WHAT YOU CAN CONNECT, that hasn't been done before.

Also, don't forget to follow accounts that share novel ideas and new AI tools to keep you inspired daily. That will help a lot as well.
Phase 2: READ, Understand the Paper in 15 Minutes
Open the paper on arXiv (free, always). Don't read it linearly. Use this order:
// Abstract (30 seconds): first sentence = problem, last sentence = claim. 

All figures and tables (2 minutes): the architecture diagram tells you 80% of what you need. 

// Introduction + Conclusion (5 minutes): what's novel and what are the honest limitations.

// Methodology (5-7 minutes): focus on the system design, skip the math. GitHub check (30 seconds): is there code? Does it run?
After this, you should be able to say: "This paper solves X by doing Y. To build it, I'd need components A, B, and C." If you can't say that, the paper is either too theoretical or you need to read the methodology section again.
// OR USE AN LLM: Upload the PDF. Let those parts given to you by your LLM in your terminal or AI chat. If something is really key to understand give it your time.

See everything like a puzzle when doing research. Each discovery, methodology, understanding, and "aha" moment is a step toward being able to guide your AI to ship something 99.9% of people won't be able to ship. Most importantly, enjoy this process. 

To enjoy it, find a product idea/discovery that you are genuinely curious about, and this will come naturally.
Phase 3: CONNECT THE PUZZLE PIECES, Seeing the Entire Landscape
This is where Connected Papers (connectedpapers.com) becomes your secret weapon. Drop your paper's arXiv URL into Connected Papers, and it builds a visual graph of related research. 
 
The free version lets you see  5 graphs a month, which should be enough, and you can explore other graphs for free.

What you're looking for: 

• the biggest darkest nodes (recent, highly-cited papers)
• tight clusters (sub-fields you could build in)
• isolated nodes at the edges (techniques from adjacent fields nobody has applied to your problem yet)
• the Prior Works (foundational papers that started this research direction) 
• and Derivative Works (the cutting-edge extensions and surveys)
 
PRO TIP: You can also build your own graphs by creating a graph directly through your own terminal as you find more interesting research papers about a field that you are researching, and update it as you find more cool discoveries on it. 

These methodologies will lead to amazing discoveries that advance the progress of mankind across many areas. I hope you are one of those who will crack the code on something worthwhile! 

The question one must ask is: ''why not?"

Every product and idea was once a figment of someone's imagination. Now, at this age, we can build them faster thanks to AI, without needing infinite resources. 
Phase 4: BUILD, Ship with Research-Backed Confidence
Now you can start to vibe code that complex thing. 

The more you understand the field, the more your prompts will differ from the generic vibe coder. 

Instead of "build me a RAG system," you'll write: "Implement a retrieval pipeline based on the RAPTOR architecture. Use recursive abstractive processing to cluster and summarize chunks into a tree. Retrieval queries against all tree levels, not just leaf nodes."
This will lead to completely different outputs. 

The specificity of your instructions, informed by your research, produces code that is architecturally distinct from what anyone else without that understanding ships.

You will guide AI better and discover beyond what it can do with just basic prompts. As most complex stuff usually needs you to guide AI, not the other way around.

Phase 5: DON'T QUIT

This phase needs little explanation, but if you're working on a hard problem, expect to be tested; it won't be easy. That will be your moat as you persist.
Part III: The Transformation Nobody Expects
Research-Driven Vibe Coding Changes YOU, Not Just Your Products.
As you grow through research-based workflows and hard problems to solve, it doesn't just produce better products:

It makes you a fundamentally better builder.
 
When you read a paper on memory architectures, you don't just learn how to build a memory system. You learn how the researchers THINK about memory. You start to understand the mind. You learn the vocabulary that lets you communicate with people working at the frontier. 

It helps you better understand other things you read in the field
And as you embark on this journey, you'll realize you haven't wasted your time on easy-to-build stuff that won't have a moat. You'll grow expertise, and that expertise will carry you onwards towards solving harder problems.

That's where the biggest opportunities are.","cover_media":"3_2038661663923355648"}
